<!-- Topic 1: Relational Operators -->
<!-- Slides 3-12 -->

# Relational Operators
<!-- Slide 3 -->

## Comparing Values {.smaller}

+ How can a program ask whether two values have a specific relationship?
+ Relational operators let code ask questions with yes-or-no answers.

::: notes
Slides 3-12
:::

<!-- Slide 4 -->

---

## The Relational Operators

| Operator | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `==` | equal to |
| `!=` | not equal to |
| `<` | less than |
| `>` | greater than |
| `<=` | less than or equal to |
| `>=` | greater than or equal to |

<!-- Slide 5 -->

---

## A Comparison Produces a Value

```cpp
int score = 87;

score >= 70
score == 100
score < 60
```

Each expression evaluates to either `true` or `false`.

<!-- Slide 6 -->

---

## Equality Is Not Assignment

```cpp
score = 70;   // Stores 70 in score
score == 70;  // Asks whether score is equal to 70
```

The single equals sign changes a variable. The double equals sign compares two values.

<!-- Slide 7 -->

---

## Common Comparison Shapes

| Shape | Example |
|---|---|
| Threshold | `age >= 21` |
| Exact choice | `choice == 1` |
| Boundary | `temperature < 32` |
| Different value | `menuChoice != 0` |

<!-- Slide 8 -->

---

## Comparing Floating-Point Values

Integers can usually be compared directly. Floating-point values may not store the exact value you expect.

```cpp
double result = .666667 * 6.0;
cout << "Printed result: " << result << endl;
```

<!-- Slide 9 -->

---

## The Comparison Problem

```cpp
double result = .666667 * 6.0;

if (result == 4.0) {
    cout << "Internal result = 4.0\n";
} else {
    cout << "Internal result != 4.0\n";
}
```

The printed value may look like 4, but the stored value may be slightly different.

<!-- Slide 10 -->

---

## Comparing with a Tolerance

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <cmath>
using namespace std;

double result = .666667 * 6.0;
double margin = 0.0001;

if (abs(result - 4.0) < margin) {
    cout << "Close enough to 4.0\n";
}
```

Decide how close is close enough before calling floating-point values equal.

<!-- Slide 11 -->

---

## Summary

- Relational operators compare values.
- A comparison evaluates to `true` or `false`.
- Decision statements use those true-or-false results to choose what code runs.

<!-- Slide 12 -->
